And perhaps at one level of taster that this is more of a parlor guessing game, and at another level that this is what a Burgophile might look for in a wine…not just a wine that is delicious and complex,but also one that speaks of a particular place. Some might argue, perhaps, that a delicious wine that does not “speak of its place” is missing something.

So that is why I ask. Not necessarily having a handle on typicity itself, and not having had enough examples of each producer’s Clos de la Eoche, for example, through different vintages, I do not have a good sense of what Clos de la Roche should taste like, and whether Ponsot’s and Leroy’s and Lignier’s and Dujac’s are different “riffs” or interpretations of CdlR but recognizable as such, in the same way that Miles Davis’ Someday My Prince Will Come is different than the Disney version but still recognizable as his interpretation of the same song. And not that I would be disappointed to hear Miles riff on any song, whether I recognized it or not…unless I specifically wanted to hear and put on SMPWC and it was unrecognizable.
Whether that matters if a wine is otherwise exciting, I do not know.

The question of individual commune identifies is an interesting one. Clearly, some communes do have some unifying features: the high percentage of active limestone in Chambolle, for example, or the predominantly south-easterly exposition of Puligny-Montrachet. But clearly, every commune also comprehends significant variation in geology / soil hydrology and altitude, as well as frequently significant variation in exposition and air-drainage. What does Meursault Les Vireuils Dessus, east-north-east facing and over 300m in elevation, with poor rocky soils below the tree line, having in common with Meursault Les Pellans, south-east facing, below 200m in elevation, on the rich soils at the bottom of Meursault Charmes? Doesn’t Vireuils Dessus have more in common with the high altitude parts of Puligny than with Meursault Les Pellans?

In the past, wines from a commune, produced by winemakers from the commune, were often made using the same methods. Deep, cold cellars in Meursault, where the water table is low, favored long fermentation and sur lie élevage in barrel; in Puligny, the shallow water table precluded that, favoring shorter élevage in larger vessels. If you needed winemaking advice to solve a problem in Puligny fifty or sixty years ago, you might ask a member of the Virot family; whereas in Meursault you might have asked René Lafon. That naturally made for more of a family resemblance between the wines of a village (even though there was clearly always more bandwidth than the caricatures allow: did François Jobard’s chiseled, austere wines ever fit the stereotype of buttery Meursault?). And when the négociants blended, they aimed to make blends that met consumer expectations for each appellation: Meursault, after all, is a brand as well as a place.

Today, as I’ve argued here before, these unifying considerations have broken down. With air conditioning and humidity control you can do a long élevage in Puligny; and with heaters in your cold cellar in Meursault, you can force your wine through alcoholic and malolactic fermentation, then filter it for bottling within a year. If you need advice, you call a consulting winemaker who may not even have come from the region. At the same time, many lieux-dits that were often blended are now bottled separately: combine Vireuils Dessus and Pellans in a blend and you might have a more “classic” Meursault; bottle them separately, and you have two very different wines. It is not that site differences have been effaced: on the contrary, micro-bottlings have multiplied the “expressions of terroir” kaleidoscopically.

All of this means that, tasting blind, it is much harder to identify the commune than it is with wines from thirty years ago. When I blind taste for fun, I try to guess the producer and the vineyard. (And sometimes, the consultant oenologist). But honestly, I find blind tasting more interesting and useful for the surprises that occur when one gets it wrong, which are an important heuristic for when I taste knowing what’s in the glass, as opposed to the satisfaction of getting it right. I loved Jim Anderson’s comments on blind tasting, by the way, everyone should read that closely.

posts.name: Jürgen Steinke
posts.cooked:
To me its simple because terroir is nothing else as soil and microclimate. The vintner is not able to change these 2 factors. They are fix. Anything else is in the hand of the winemaker. It is impossible to produce a superb wine if the soil does not fit the conditions of the vines. And if the micro climate is too hot, too cool, too wet or too dry the results will not be satisfying. It is true that a microclimate can be pretty different from one mile to the next or even half a mile. And the soil often changes within a vineyard. To know and respect all these details is the art of a passionate vintner. The french classification system tries to mirror these differences in terroir. Especially in Burgundy. And many wine lovers are fascinated by the endless number of small vineyards. Those who can name all of them or almost all are the people in the know, the professors, the elite. This makes Burgundy special. Barolo and Barbaresco is easy to understand in comparison. Not to speak about Bordeaux which is simple.

The only question is: What is more important – the terroir or the wine maker? The terroirists believe the terroir is most important. I do not believe that this is true. Why? Because the most competent and busy wine makers in Burgundy often produces better wines from village level terroir than other wine makers at Premier or even Grand Cru land. The same is true almost everywhere. How can this happen? This is easy to explain. While one vintner works very hard in the vineyard the next one does his work not with the same passion. While one wine maker has a better palate than the next and is able to find a wonderful balance because he does exactly the right things in the cellar by talent and/or by experience. In comparison: some chefs are technically perfect in the kitchen but not good in seasoning the food.

Because all things are very complex, one factor alone can not define wines of a certain origin. I bet: Take one of the finest Burgundy wine maker and let him make wine in Baden/Germany (Pinot Land). Give him time, the same Burgundy clones and the same equipment. Let him choose the vineyard. And I am pretty sure the results will be very similar to what he produced in France after a while. Why do I believe this? More and more young and talented German winemakers were educated at top Burgundy Domains. If they are given carte blanche from the older generations at home their wines taste more and more like Burgundies.

Sure: some people cannot and will not believe this. But it is my experience. I once talked with Michel Bettane about this. He said more or less that people who deny that fine Pinots comparable to those of Burgundy could not be produced elsewhere have prejudices or a lack of tasting experience. And what I learned over the 40 years I taste and drink wine is: never underestimate the power of imagination and myth.

posts.name: Marcus Goodfellow
posts.cooked:
Man, I tried to stay out of this.

The concept of terroir is innately difficult to both define and to grasp, the smaller the scale that one attempts in which to define it can seem to disprove it and attempting to show it in specific tastings, as Mel notes above, is nigh well impossible. That does not mean it doesn’t exist or that it’s some sort of marketing scheme. There are tons of things in this world that we deal with every day that actually are not that well understood or that have different theories or completely different sides to them. Climate change (okay, that’s perhaps political or has been made that way). The dynamics of how bikes work. Infinity. The human brain. Love. Where the other sock in the dryer goes. And on and on and on.

At a bedrock level I would say that terroir is a lot like pornography, I may not be able to tell you what it is but I know it when I see it. It’s not mysticism or hokum or faith but it’s also not exactly science where 1+1+1=3 (incidentally that is harder than you think, the proof as to why 1+1=2 is like 200 pages long). That’s why you can’t take Chambolle from one producer and pour it next to Chambolle from another and magically find Chambolle-ness blasting out of the glasses. Life is harder than that.

If you read about romance, or god forbid, a romance novel it sounds ridiculous as a concept or something put into practice at any sort of reasonable level. Terroir is sort of like that. Describing it in technical terms is futile because the science behind it is some combination of not well understood (if you think we have a complete understanding of plants you are WAY off base), not very specific and incomplete. Describing terroir in more ethereal terms sounds like you’re full of shit or selling snake oil.

Does this mean terroir is somehow bunk? I guess that depends on you. If you need terroir to make an appearance for you when you’re looking for it you are most likely going to be sadly disappointed or annoyed. What good does this all do for you if you want to know about it? Probably pretty f-ing little. Sorry.

However, as someone that produces 30 bottlings of Pinot Noir a year and as many as 6 from a couple of different vineyards I obviously believe in the concept and our ability to show it somehow. I can talk about soils, soil depth, root growth, vine age, clones, microclimates and so on as to how and why different vineyards or sections of vineyard lend their influence to why the wine is the way it is. Maybe that will resonate with you and maybe it won’t.

I believe in it, see it, feel it and taste it. But I don’t know that I can describe it in a satisfactory sort of way.

I started off saying that it’s bullshit and faith. Jim, I respect your nuanced approach, that it may be there, but it’s not something objective and scientific.

Yes and no. The “sense of place” is not objective or scientific, in my opinion.

The differences in grape physiology between sites is both objective and scientific.

Tannins at Whistling Ridge have always been more prominent in the fruit at Whistling Ridge vs. the fruit at Durant in the 10 years I have made wines from both.

Sugar at harvest is always lower at Whistling Ridge than Durant. That’s regardless of hot vintage or cool.

500 years ago, basic things would have stood out much more so than they do today. And as I noted, we changed the farming process to rein in the fruit at Durant, and alter the elevage of each wine to accommodate both.

As Jim noted, we taste these wines at a vastly higher rate than most people, including eating the fruit, and prior to them having any influence of barrel aging-but ONLY these wines. So for a list of 15-25 wines, the terroir is fairly obvious to me. And for 20-30 wines, it’s that way for Jim.

Regarding Jim’s comment on blind tasting, it’s easy to divert ourselves with too many guesses. At the winery, at lunch I often blind taste my crew on a wine from my personal cellar, or occasionally our own wines. They are very good tasters, and often can tell me if a wine is one of ours. But they also do a remarkably good job of working through blinds from other places, and the typical place the tasting derails is when they start to think about their choices. But that’s all within a framework of degrees of success. There are days no one is close, and days they do very well. Compared to random, they actually do pretty well with old world-new world and varietal. Region is more hit or miss, but climbs remarkably if it’s WV Pinot Noir or Chardonnay. Vineyard or producer is never guessed unless it’s our wine, or maybe another WV producer with a distinct style(Eyrie, Cristom, PGC) that we taste regularly. And this is just a single wine rather than a flight.

I don’t think an understanding of world wide terroir is within many peoples grasp. But if you visit the Willamette Valley, you should visit with Will Hamilton at Violin-he worked for a winery producing wine for many clients when he started, then did a stint in filtration for local wineries, and then worked for a winemaking supply business while he was getting his winery, Violin, off the ground. He has a remarkable palate and a deeper than most experience with a broad range of WV wines. You should also visit Patricia Green Cellars and myself.

In the whole thread, probably the best advice on easiest way for you to make it less objective and more scientific-and to see the concept clearly-is to taste through younger wines with a single producer. And having gone back to read Mr. Kelley’s post, the wines of a village have less in common than they used to.

Jim and I have very different cellar signatures on our wines. And those are impactful. If you don’t have a familiarity with our cellar signatures then the influence from site is going to be very difficult to pick out. But if you come and taste with me, I have very little doubt that you will see the nature of the sites that I am talking about.

What does Meursault Les Vireuils Dessus, east-north-east facing and over 300m in elevation, with poor rocky soils below the tree line, having in common with Meursault Les Pellans, south-east facing, below 200m in elevation, on the rich soils at the bottom of Meursault Charmes? Doesn’t Vireuils Dessus have more in common with the high altitude parts of Puligny than with Meursault Les Pellans?

In the past, wines from a commune, produced by winemakers from the commune, were often made using the same methods. Deep, cold cellars in Meursault, where the water table is low, favored long fermentation and sur lie élevage in barrel; in Puligny, the shallow water table precluded that, favoring shorter élevage in larger vessels. If you needed winemaking advice to solve a problem in Puligny fifty or sixty years ago, you might ask a member of the Virot family; whereas in Meursault you might have asked René Lafon. That naturally made for more of a family resemblance between the wines of a village (even though there was clearly always more bandwidth than the caricatures allow: did François Jobard’s chiseled, austere wines ever fit the stereotype of buttery Meursault?). And when the négociants blended, they aimed to make blends that met consumer expectations for each appellation: Meursault, after all, is a brand as well as a place.

Today, as I’ve argued here before, these unifying considerations have broken down. With air conditioning and humidity control you can do a long élevage in Puligny; and with heaters in your cold cellar in Meursault, you can force your wine through alcoholic and malolactic fermentation, then filter it for bottling within a year. If you need advice, you call a consulting winemaker who may not even have come from the region. At the same time, many lieux-dits that were often blended are now bottled separately: combine Vireuils Dessus and Pellans in a blend and you might have a more “classic” Meursault; bottle them separately, and you have two very different wines. It is not that site differences have been effaced: on the contrary, micro-bottlings have multiplied the “expressions of terroir” kaleidoscopically.

All of this means that, tasting blind, it is much harder to identify the commune than it is with wines from thirty years ago. When I blind taste for fun, I try to guess the producer and the vineyard. (And sometimes, the consultant oenologist). But honestly, I find blind tasting more interesting and useful for the surprises that occur when one gets it wrong, which are an important heuristic for when I taste knowing what’s in the glass, as opposed to the satisfaction of getting it right. I loved Jim Anderson’s comments on blind tasting, by the way, everyone should read that closely.

This is a great response. Though I’ve often thought I had a very modest handle on some basic characteristics of one commune vs another, I also recognize that there is wide variation in soils, exposition, micro-climate, etc. for certain sections over others within a single commune. To me that actually strengthens the argument for terroir: that those variations often result in distinctly different wines, grown and vinified similarly by the same producer. So the argument that even wines across a single commune don’t always have obvious commonality supports the notion of terroir more than undercuts it.

Yes, and when you consider that the commune boundaries were often determined more by historical happenstance or convenience than by geological or geographical distinctions, you can’t rely too much on just the village. For example, Nuits-Boudots is on the border with Vosne and tends to show more like a Vosne than most Nuits.

And perhaps at one level of taster that this is more of a parlor guessing game, and at another level that this is what a Burgophile might look for in a wine…not just a wine that is delicious and complex,but also one that speaks of a particular place. Some might argue, perhaps, that a delicious wine that does not “speak of its place” is missing something.

So that is why I ask. Not necessarily having a handle on typicity itself, and not having had enough examples of each producer’s Clos de la Eoche, for example, through different vintages, I do not have a good sense of what Clos de la Roche should taste like, and whether Ponsot’s and Leroy’s and Lignier’s and Dujac’s are different “riffs” or interpretations of CdlR but recognizable as such, in the same way that Miles Davis’ Someday My Prince Will Come is different than the Disney version but still recognizable as his interpretation of the same song. And not that I would be disappointed to hear Miles riff on any song, whether I recognized it or not…unless I specifically wanted to hear and put on SMPWC and it was unrecognizable.

Fascinating!

Many years back, there was some research suggesting that ambient yeasts accounted for some of the difference between communes in Bordeaux. Do you think there’s anything to that? I realize there’s a lot of commercial yeast everywhere, and individual cellars could have unique yeast cultures, so perhaps that isn’t a viable explanation for some of the village-to-village variations.

posts.cooked:

I realize there’s a lot of commercial yeast everywhere, and individual cellars could have unique yeast cultures, so perhaps that isn’t a viable explanation for some of the village-to-village variations.

People will go back and forth on the influence of yeast, I think, but I suspect that if each commune did have some sort of distinctive microbiome (built up over centuries of dumping the pomace from successful ferments back on the vineyards, as the old Italian winemakers used to do in California), that would have been lost overnight with the introduction of synthetic fungicides.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
I should have been clearer: my point was that they are not second-class wines that can’t conformably sit on the same table as the best wines of the Côte de Nuits. I suspect you’d agree with that. I can think of plenty of instances where top wines of the Côte de Beaune rival or surpass top wines of the Côte de Nuits: put 1989 La Tâche up against Lafarge’s 1989 Clos des Chênes, for example, or the 1985 Pousse d’Or 60 Ouvrées against Jean Gros’ 1985 Richebourg. I remember a Leroy Corton-Bressandes 1949 dominating the Richebourg of the same year. These things happen often enough to dispel prejudices against the wines of the Côte de Beaune. And what’s more, the red wines of the Côte de Beaune today are arguably better than they have been for decades.

And I should have given more love to CdB in my response, because many wines can be superb. Still, if price were no factor, I’d probably buy 10:1 CdN over CdB for red wines. As it is, it’s probably 1:1, due to the quality I can get for the price.

I agree with you, if price were no object I would buy considerably more CdN than CdB, but a number of years ago I was facilitating a vertical tasting of Richebourg going back to 1970 for a group. I was fortunate to taste the line up, including wines from DRC and Anne Gros. One of the tasters, a person I had known a while, tapped me at one point then pulled up a bottle from under the table and requested I taste it. 1990 Pousse d’Or Clos de 60 Ouvres and my wine of the tasting.
(Not suggesting this is a revelation to you Alan. More a corroboration of your point. And perhaps a musing on how lucky I have been in being gifted so many great opportunities to taste wines I would not have chosen myself before being exposed to them).

Marcus, it’s interesting that you mention that Pousse 60 Ouvres bottling. I can’t remember what older vintages I may have had, not many, but I have bought a fair number of bottles of that wine in more recent vintages. I actually decided a few years ago that it’s a “bigger” wine than I prefer, which I have found to be a general description of the Pousse wines, at least going back to 2002 vintage, or maybe longer. I assume the 90 was made by Gerard Potel, which might explain the differences. Anyway, I no longer buy Pousse d’Or wines, just a little “too much” for my tastes.

posts.name: Nick Christie
posts.cooked:
Just want to say this single page of this thread is one of the best discussions of wine I’ve read in quite some time and thanks to the contributors.

Indeed. Once this thread really opened up and went full throttle, it’s been great to get out of the way and listen. Memorable and Powerful.

posts.name: Wes Barton
posts.cooked:
Many years back, there was some research suggesting that ambient yeasts accounted for some of the difference between communes in Bordeaux. Do you think there’s anything to that? I realize there’s a lot of commercial yeast everywhere, and individual cellars could have unique yeast cultures, so perhaps that isn’t a viable explanation for some of the village-to-village variations.

More than that, now. Some of the microbes on a grape’s surface may interact with the ripening grape and influence the creation of compounds or precursor compounds. The population on a grape’s surface are competing and cooperating, the make-up adapting to the ever-changing conditions. So, you have both their impact on the grapes themselves on the vine, and in the early ferment. Some microbes will be more sensitive to varying conditions than others, so some will be broadly impactful, while the impact of others will be subject to picking decision, vintage variations, canopy management, etc. So, the same microbe that may be responsible for a general aspect of the terroir of one site may be an aspect of vintage variation of another.

This is one of the clearest, most detailed, and most lucid examples I have ever read of how sociological and technological factors determine “terroir”. Thanks William, very illuminating. I wish critics spent much more time describing these details of how winemaking practices and cultural/economic factors determine wine styles and their evolution, instead of spending all their time racking their brains for more over-the-top flavor descriptors to put in giant lists.

Heh. You could put all sorts of effort and money into introducing stuff, but there’s no guarantee they would survive, compete or make a difference at another site. Better, would just be fostering a healthy ecosystem in which a complex array of beneficial microbes can thrive. Just like you want a healthy soil biome (soil web), you want habitat in and around the vineyard to increase microbial diversity, house and attract beneficial insects, etc. Some may also impart aromatic compounds into or onto the grapes.

This is why I believe a strong biodiversity in the vineyard is more important than routine inoculations (such as with biodynamics). It encourages a strong soil biome. We are discovering how important a good biome is in medicine as well as agriculture.

Agreed. If you have a healthy gut biome, it makes no sense to take probiotic supplements. That’s a set of the same isolated strains over and over. A diverse, balanced diet that includes nutrients for you, your gut biome, and the random introduction of new beneficial microbes is great to maintain.

Soil inoculations make sense to jump-start an unhealthy site, along with other measures. I’ve never done that, but I did French double dig improvements into my awful heavy black clay 30 years ago two years in a row. No till since and it’s fine. (Btw, compost worms absolutely love pommace, which creates a population boom of highly intoxicated worms.)
